advantages: Maps any real input to a smooth, differentiable output in (0, 1) — ideal for probabilities and multiplicative gates.
Monotonic and bounded: outputs are easy to interpret as confidence or gate strength without clipping heuristics.
Simple closed-form derivative σ′(x) = σ(x)(1−σ(x)) — cheap to implement in manual backprop exercises.
Well-studied with decades of theory in statistics (logit models) and early neural-network literature.
Still the right default for binary output neurons and RNN/LSTM gates where (0, 1) bounds are required.
disadvantages: Saturation at |x| large drives σ′(x)→0, causing vanishing gradients when used in deep hidden layers.
Not zero-centered (outputs always positive), which can slow optimization compared to tanh or ReLU in hidden layers.
Maximum derivative 0.25 is four times smaller than tanh's peak and much smaller than ReLU's active-region gradient.
Computationally heavier than ReLU (exponential) with little benefit in modern deep feedforward stacks.
Can produce systematically biased activations in hidden layers, pushing subsequent units toward saturation.
